title: The part that clings to toxic relationships for familiar pain
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The part that keeps you in rooms that hurt you

There's a part of you that keeps going back. I know it because I've felt it too. That pull toward people who don't treat you well. That strange comfort in the familiar sting of being dismissed, overlooked, or worse. You know the relationship is toxic. Your friends tell you. Maybe you've even left before. But something inside you drags you back, or keeps you frozen in place, unable to walk away.

Here's the thing: that part isn't broken. It isn't stupid. It isn't self-destructive for the sake of being difficult. That part is trying to protect you from something even worse than the pain you already know. And until you understand what that something is, you'll keep choosing the devil you know over the devil you don't.

I want to talk about this part directly. The one that clings to toxic relationships because the pain is familiar. The one that mistakes intensity for love. The one that would rather stay in a burning house than face the cold dark outside. Because honestly, that part is doing a job. A very specific, very old job. And it's been doing it since long before you had any say in the matter.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, talks about how the nervous system gets stuck in patterns of survival. When you grow up in an environment where love is conditional, where affection comes with strings, where safety is unpredictable, your system learns that this is what connection looks like. Your body encodes that information at a level far below conscious thought. So when you find yourself in a similar dynamic as an adult, something inside you says "finally, I know how to do this." Even if "this" is terrible for you.

Think about it. Your nervous system doesn't care about happiness. It cares about prediction. It cares about knowing what's coming next. Because predictability equals safety, even when what's predictable is painful. A known pain is manageable. An unknown situation, even a potentially good one, feels like a threat. So that part of you clings to the toxic relationship because at least you know the script. You know when the explosion will come. You know how to apologize. You know how to shrink. You know how to survive.

Wild, right? The part that looks like it's keeping you stuck is actually trying to keep you alive.

Where this part was born

This part didn't appear out of nowhere. It was forged in the fires of your early life, in the specific ways you learned to adapt to environments that weren't safe enough. If you grew up with a parent who was emotionally unpredictable, who swung between warmth and coldness, who required you to earn their love, then your developing brain made a logical conclusion: love looks like this. Connection requires suffering. Closeness comes with a price.

Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," has spent decades showing how early relational trauma shapes the developing brain and nervous system. When you're a child and your caregiver is also your source of fear, your system faces an impossible paradox. You need them to survive, but they're also the source of threat. So your brain develops a strategy: attach to the caregiver no matter what, even if that attachment is painful. Because the alternative, complete disconnection, feels like death to a child's nervous system.

That strategy doesn't disappear when you turn eighteen. It doesn't disappear when you move out. It doesn't disappear when you swear you'll never date someone like your parent again. That strategy is etched into your neural pathways, into your body's survival responses. And when you meet someone who activates those same old patterns, that part of you lights up. Finally. Someone who makes sense. Someone who feels like home. Even if home was a war zone.

I've sat with this part in myself. It's not loud. It doesn't scream. It whispers. It says things like "but they love me really" and "they didn't mean it" and "I just need to try harder." It makes excuses. It rationalizes. It points to the good moments and uses them as evidence that the bad moments aren't real. It's a master at gaslighting you into staying.

And it's not evil. It's terrified. It's the part of you that remembers what it felt like to be a small child who couldn't leave, who couldn't say no, who had to make the best of a bad situation because there was no other option. That part doesn't know you're an adult now. It doesn't know you have resources, choices, a car, a bank account, friends who will help you pack. It still thinks you're that helpless kid who has to survive by any means necessary.

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty (paid link). It names the invisible ways we were taught that our feelings don't matter, which is often the foundation this part is built on.

How this part operates in your body

This part isn't just a thought pattern. It lives in your body. When you're in a toxic relationship, notice what happens physically when you consider leaving. Your chest might tighten. Your throat might close. Your stomach might drop. Your hands might get cold. That's the part activating your nervous system's freeze or fawn response, flooding you with sensations that feel like evidence that leaving is dangerous.

I've had clients describe it as a physical pull. Like a magnetic force dragging them back toward someone who hurts them. Like their body won't let them walk away. That's not weakness. That's your survival brain doing exactly what it was designed to do. The problem is that your survival brain was designed for a different world, a different time, a different version of you.

Janina Fisher, who integrates Internal Family Systems with trauma treatment, talks about how these parts are not pathologies to be eliminated but protectors to be understood. The part that clings to toxic relationships is a protector. It's protecting you from the terror of being alone. From the shame of having chosen wrong. From the grief of acknowledging that someone you love doesn't love you back the way you deserve. From the existential dread of not knowing who you are without this person defining you.

Stay with me here. This is the hard part. When you start to separate from this part, when you start to question its strategy, it will fight back. It will flood you with doubt. It will remind you of all the good times. It will make you feel guilty for even considering leaving. It will convince you that you're being too sensitive, too demanding, too much. That's the part's job. It's trying to keep you in the familiar because the familiar feels safe, even when it's destroying you.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link). Richard Schwartz, who created Internal Family Systems, explains that every part has a positive intention, even the ones that seem to be working against us.

What this part needs from you

This part doesn't need to be shamed, silenced, or forced into submission. It needs to be heard. It needs you to acknowledge its fear. It needs you to say "I see that you're trying to protect me. I understand that you're scared. And I'm going to take it from here."

That's the work. Not fighting the part. Not pretending it doesn't exist. Not judging yourself for still being in a situation that's bad for you. But turning toward the part with curiosity and compassion, the way you would toward a frightened child who's hiding under the bed.

What is this part afraid would happen if you left? What catastrophe is it trying to prevent? What would be the worst thing about being alone, about not having this person in your life? The answers might surprise you. For many of us, the deepest fear is not about the other person at all. It's about what we would have to feel if we weren't distracted by the chaos of a toxic relationship. The grief. The loneliness. The rage. The despair that we never got what we needed.

Sit with that. The part might be clinging to the toxic relationship because the relationship itself is less painful than the emptiness it covers up. The drama, the intensity, the constant state of hypervigilance, these are all ways to avoid the quieter, deeper pain that's been there all along. The pain that predates the relationship. The pain that was there before you ever met this person.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman. She was one of the first to name how trauma lives in relationship and can only be healed in relationship, including the relationship you have with yourself and your parts.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) by Lindsay Gibson. It helps you see that the part clinging to toxic relationships was trained in childhood to accept less than you deserved.

What it looks like to work with this part

The goal isn't to get rid of this part. The goal is to help it trust that you can handle something new. That you can survive discomfort without a familiar source of pain to anchor you. That you are no longer the helpless child who had no choice.

This looks like small experiments. Maybe you don't leave the relationship today. But you notice the part when it shows up. You name it. You say "there's the part that's scared to be alone." You breathe into the sensation in your chest. You stay with it without acting on it. That's already different. That's already a new response.

Maybe you start setting one small boundary and survive the discomfort. Maybe you tell the person "I can't talk right now" and notice that the world doesn't end. Maybe you spend an evening alone and discover that you don't actually die of loneliness. Each small victory builds trust with this part. It starts to learn that you can handle things it never thought you could.

I'm not going to tell you that this is easy. It's not. Some days the part will be louder than you. Some days you'll go back to the same old patterns and feel like you've made no progress at all. That's part of the process. Healing is not linear. It's more like a spiral. You circle back to the same issues but from a slightly different angle each time, with a little more awareness, a little more capacity.

Notice what just happened in your body as you read that. Did something relax? Did something tighten? That's the part responding. It's listening. It's watching to see if you're going to be different this time.

Be different this time. Not by force. Not by willpower. But by presence. By the part instead of against it. By recognizing that the part that clings to toxic relationships is not your enemy. It's your most loyal soldier, still fighting a war that ended years ago. You can thank it for its service. You can tell it that you're safe now. You can show it, slowly, painfully, imperfectly, that you can handle something new.

And then you can start to let go.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Running on Empty (paid link) - For understanding emotional neglect and its impact on your relationship patterns.
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - A gentle introduction to Internal Family Systems and working with all your parts.
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link) - Judith Herman's foundational text on understanding trauma and its healing.
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) - Validates childhood experiences that create the blueprint for toxic adult relationships.

As an Amazon Associate, I earn from qualifying purchases.
